Off Calendar Feb-18-2021 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: (PART 2 OF 2)
(Mini Minutes continued from previous entry)

Additionally, Intervenors' argument regarding Plaintiff's factually devoid document production and witness identification is unpersuasive. Intervenors have failed to discuss any documents produced by Plaintiff in detail, and do not address the multiple co-workers identified by Plaintiff as witnesses who may have additional information. (See James Decl., Ex. D, Response to Interrogatory No. 5.) Ultimately, Intervenors' argument fails to explain how Plaintiff's discovery responses support the contention that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, evidence proving the element of causation. (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 103, citing Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield, supra, 25 Cal.4th 826, Saelzler v. Advanced Group 400 (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763 [""[A] defendant cannot simply 'argue' that a plaintiff lacks sufficient evidence to establish causation; the defendant must make an affirmative 'showing' that the plaintiff cannot do so"].)

Lastly, Intervenors reliance on Smith v. AC and S (1994) 31 Cal.App.4th 77 (Smith) and Chaknova v. Wilbur-Ellis (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 962 (Chaknova) is unavailing. In Smith, the Court of Appeal noted that in order to prove causation with respect to asbestos-exposure, "there must be proof that the defendant's asbestos products or activities were present at plaintiff's worksite." (Smith v. AC and S (1994) 31 Cal.App.4th 77, 89.) As noted above, it is undisputed between the parties that Plaintiff was present while J&H employees removed and installed alleged asbestos-containing flooring material upon eight (8) ships at Plaintiff's worksite. (See Intervenors' Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, No. 7-8.) Further, the reasoning employed in Chaknova has been expressly disapproved by the Supreme Court. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co., supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 854-855, fn. 23; Weber v. John Crane, supra, 143 Cal.App.4th at p. 1441.) Accordingly, Intervenors' Motion for Summary Judgment is denied. Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Not reported. (503/CML)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Feb-18-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Jon C. James of HUGO & PARKER (415-808-0300), appearing through CourtCall for Intervenors and moving parties National Union Fire Insurance Company of Pittsburgh, PA, TIG Insurance Company, Certain Underwriters at Lloyd's London, and Certain London Market Insurance Companies.
Andrew Chew of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing through CourtCall for plaintiffs and moving parties Mark Owens and Debora Owens.

RULING:
Not contested and tentative ruling adopted: Intervenors National Union Fire Insurance Company of Pittsburgh, PA, TIG Insurance Company, Certain Underwriters at Lloyd's London, and Certain London Market Insurance Companies' Motion for Summary Judgment is denied. Intervenors National Union Fire Insurance Company of Pittsburgh, PA, TIG Insurance Company, Certain Underwriters at Lloyd's London, and Certain London Market Insurance Companies ("Intervenors") have failed to sustain their initial burden of production pursuant to Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 854-855. It is undisputed between the parties that Plaintiff was present while J&H Marine & Industrial Engineering Company employees ("J&H employees") removed and installed alleged asbestos-containing flooring material upon eight (8) ships at Plaintiff's worksite, Triple A Machine Shop. (See Intervenors' Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, No. 7-8.) The relevant issue upon consideration of Intervenors' Motion is whether Intervenors have produced evidence proving that "plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence" to establish that the activities of J&H employees exposed Plaintiff to asbestos. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co., supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 854.) Intervenors have failed to make this showing. The evidence submitted along with Intervenors' Motion, specifically Plaintiffs' Responses to Intervenors' Special Interrogatories, include specific facts about the way in which Plaintiff was exposed to asbestos while working as a pipefitter upon the eight (8) ships. (See James Decl., Ex. D, Response to Interrogatory No. 5.) Further, while Intervenors rely on Plaintiff's deposition testimony wherein he states that he has "no idea" whether the materials used or removed by J&H employees contained asbestos, it cannot be inferred from Plaintiff's own ignorance that Plaintiff will be unable to produce other evidence or testimony proving asbestos-content. (See Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.) The same reasoning is employed to rebut Intervenors' argument regarding Plaintiff's inability to remember the specific names of the eight (8) ships where Plaintiff remembers working alongside J&H employees.
